cataloging_subject.label: The South English Ministry and Passion (Laud Misc. 108 Fragment)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The 901 surviving Middle English verse lines of the Laud Misc. 108 witness of the anonymous 3,048-line South English Ministry and Passion, as first printed by Carl Horstmann in 1873 and encoded in CME00148. The item includes Horstmann's title page, 204 editorial footnotes, and a closing note, but not his pp. 1-28 German introduction. One footnote to relative line 761 transcribes a 58-line passage from the distinct South English Legendary Life of St Mary Magdalene in the same codex, ff. 191r-v; that quoted paratext is modeled as a nested content part and related work. Its lines are included in the whole-item representation extent and separately scoped as quoted Middle English, but they do not change the intrinsic form or discovery facets of the cataloged Ministry and Passion fragment. The target fragment begins and ends imperfectly and has an internal lacuna of about 272 lines between manuscript ff. 1 and 2. The abstract work, fragmentary Laud witness, fuller St John's B.6 witness, collateral Trinity R.3.25 extract, related Magdalene quotation, scholarly editions, and digital representations remain distinct. Horstmann's editorial title Leben Jesu and title-page reference to Kindheit Jesu do not make this a generic Life of Christ or an encoding of the separate Infancy poem.

catalog_summary.title: The South English Ministry and Passion (Laud Misc. 108 Fragment)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed before 1290; directly attested in Laud Misc. 108 around 1300; fragment first published in 1873; complete work first published in 1984
catalog_summary.region: England; the place of composition is unknown, while the Laud copy's Hand A is localized to west Oxfordshire

genre_statements.label: Gospel paraphrase
notes: Pickering contrasts MP's comparatively casual gospel paraphrases with the Southern Passion reviser's closer Vulgate translations.

subject_statements.label: Passion of Christ
notes: The Laud/CME fragment ends during Judas's arrangement for betrayal, before most of the complete work's Passion narrative.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English narrative of Christ's public ministry, Passion, Resurrection, and the disciples' later activity, comprising 3,048 lines in septenary rhyming couplets. It was probably designed as a sequel to The Nativity of Mary and Christ. The later Southern Passion carefully revises it, often replacing its freer gospel paraphrase with closer Vulgate translation and adding expository material. CME00148 preserves only the 901-line Laud fragment within Horstmann's editorial frame; one footnote quotes 58 lines of a distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene. That passage is excluded from the target work's discovery facets but modeled separately and included in the encoded item's representation extent.

scope.description: The abstract work represented only in part by CME00148.

summaries.value: The surviving Laud portion opens mid-episode with Jesus healing a deaf-mute, then recounts miracles, teachings and parables, the Transfiguration, Lazarus, the entry into Jerusalem, the cleansing of the Temple, and opposition from Jewish leaders. It breaks off while Judas considers the thirty pieces associated with betraying Jesus. Both boundaries are incomplete, and an internal loss follows the first folio.

CME00148 contains Horstmann's edition title page and printed pp. 29-69, including footnotes and the closing report of a Latin manuscript direction. It omits the 28-page German introduction. The title page names both Leben Jesu and Kindheit Jesu, but the encoded body contains only the first-part Ministry and Passion fragment; the separate Infancy poem is not encoded. The note to relative line 761 quotes 58 lines of the separate Life of St Mary Magdalene from Laud ff. 191r-v, so that passage is retained as identified edition paratext rather than merged into the target work.

extent.description: Five modeled content parts (four top-level and one nested): one seven-paragraph edition title page; one LG containing 903 direct L elements, of which the opening and closing rows are dotted lacuna markers and 901 are verbal target-work lines; an apparatus of 204 NOTE elements; a zero-item nested part for the 58 L elements that quote the distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene inside one of those notes; and one TAILNOTE transcribing and explaining a Latin manuscript direction. The 207 item total is therefore 1 title page + 1 target fragment + 204 notes + 0 separately numbered quotation items + 1 closing note. Whole-item verse extent is 959 verbal lines (901 target-work lines + 58 quoted lines), while the two dotted display rows are excluded; the raw XML has 961 L elements. The quote's zero item count prevents double-counting its containing footnote, so the form totals remain two prose parts, one verse item, and one mixed apparatus. Forty-two PB elements comprise the title page plus printed body pp. 29-69; the complete two-page Magdalene note is serialized before the PB for printed p. 62.

content_parts.label: Horstmann's footnote apparatus
content_parts.summary: Two hundred four German-led footnotes recording manuscript readings, proposed corrections, linguistic parallels, and biblical citations. The note to relative target line 761 includes a coherent 58-line Middle English transcription from the distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene in Laud 108, ff. 191r-v.

locators.value: 204 NOTE[@PLACE='foot'] elements distributed through the target LG
footnotes throughout printed pp. 29-69 = PDF pp. 41-81

notes: German framing and brief Latin citations remain edition-layer description rather than work-language facets.
The separately modeled child quotation prevents its related-work, witness, and facsimile sources from being attributed to all 204 notes.

content_parts.label: Quoted passage from the Life of St Mary Magdalene
content_parts.summary: A coherent 58-line Middle English verse passage from the distinct South English Legendary Life of St Mary Magdalene, transcribed by Horstmann from Laud Misc. 108, ff. 191r-v, inside his footnote to Ministry and Passion relative line 761.

notes: item_count 0 records that this is a passage inside one of the 204 already counted footnotes, not an additional numbered item; its 58 verbal lines remain included in whole-item verse extent.
The passage is edition paratext and not a section of the cataloged Ministry and Passion fragment.
Because the XML nests the entire two-page quotation before PB 62, the source PDF rather than XML PB position supplies its internal page boundary.

content_parts.label: Closing manuscript note and Horstmann explanation
content_parts.summary: A German editorial statement transcribes the later Latin direction at the foot of Laud f. 10v, which tells the reader to turn to a sign near the book's beginning for more of the Passion after the Assumption of Mary; Horstmann explains that this continuation was lost with the manuscript's beginning.

locators.value: printed p. 69 = PDF p. 81, after relative line 901
bottom margin of f. 10v; complete-facsimile canvas 30

notes: The note is transmission evidence about lost or misbound material, not a surviving second work in CME00148.

related_works.title: The Nativity of Mary and Christ
notes: DIMEV describes the Ministry and Passion as a continuation of record 6380; Pickering considers it probably written as that work's sequel. St John's B.6 places the Nativity immediately before the Ministry and Passion.

related_works.title: The Southern Passion
notes: The Southern Passion is a later revision of the Ministry and Passion, not its source; it adds closer Vulgate translation and other material.

related_works.title: The South English Legendary
notes: The Ministry and Passion belongs to the associated temporale narrative tradition; applying a single fixed South English Legendary structure remains a modern scholarly convenience.

related_works.title: Life of St Mary Magdalene (South English Legendary version)
notes: Horstmann's note to Ministry and Passion relative line 761 transcribes 58 lines from this distinct work, beginning at his Magdalene line 82, from Laud ff. 191r-v. The full Laud copy occupies ff. 190r-197r. The quotation is edition paratext, not a section of the cataloged Ministry and Passion fragment.

related_works.title: The Apocryphal History of the Infancy of Jesus Christ
notes: This distinct poem follows at Laud ff. 11r-22v and is named by Horstmann's broader title, but it is not encoded in CME00148's body and is not part of the Ministry and Passion.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, Leben Jesu, ein Fragment, und Kindheit Jesu, I. Theil: Leben Jesu (Münster, 1873).

evidence.summary: Defines the 1873 edition and fragment title, identifies Laud 108, counts 901 target lines, describes losses and contents, prints the target on pp. 29-69, and documents supplied punctuation, capitalized names, and expanded abbreviations. Its note to target line 761 explicitly quotes 58 lines beginning with Magdalene line 82 from Laud f. 191.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.locator: Ministry and Passion ff. 1r-10v; St Mary Magdalene ff. 190r-197r; physical description and Hand A
evidence.summary: Supplies the established target-work title, DIMEV 4633, 901-line count, full-work line boundaries 874-2047, 272-line internal loss, Horstmann pages, manuscript date, and later directing note. It separately identifies the Life of St Mary Magdalene at ff. 190r-197r as DIMEV 4928; the cached catalog TEI and its fixity are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF manifest for MS Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.locator: target ff. 1r-10v = canvases 9-30; supporting Magdalene quotation ff. 191r-v = canvases 397-398
evidence.summary: Verifies the complete visual witness, exact target and supporting-quotation folio/canvas mappings, physical lacuna surfaces, and provider date statement. The complete private bundle uses the effective width-only profile 1600, for all 496 JPEG images.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, records 4633 (NIMEV 2931.55) and 4928 (NIMEV 3159).
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-4633 and witnesses 1, 2, and 5; record-4928 and witness 1; Manuscripts.xml BodLauMis108, CamStJohn28, CamTCC605
evidence.summary: Defines the target as a temporale narrative of 3,048 lines arranged in couplets and a continuation of record 6380, supplies full-work and witness incipits, all three witnesses, exact folios, Trinity's reported 52-line extent and line-427-472 mapping, bibliographic editions, and witness dialect localizations. Record 4928 identifies the distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene and its Laud copy at ff. 190r-197r.
notes: The exact cached Zenodo URL returned HTTP 403 in one anonymous scripted audit and HTTP 200 during a later check; the DOI and verified cache establish public release access.

evidence.citation: Oliver S. Pickering, 'The Southern Passion and the Ministry and Passion: The Work of a Middle English Reviser,' Leeds Studies in English, n.s. 15 (1984): 33-56.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 33-36 = PDF pp. 2-5; printed pp. 51-56 = PDF pp. 20-25
evidence.summary: Establishes the direction of revision from Ministry and Passion to Southern Passion, the gospel-paraphrase character, the early-fifteenth-century B.6 date, B.6 as sole witness for most of the work, and exact locations of all three witnesses. The 25-page provider PDF extracts completely; qpdf's recoverable linearization hint warnings are documented in lineage.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive controlled-access scan metadata for Oliver S. Pickering, The South English Ministry and Passion (1984).
evidence.summary: Confirms the 256-page first full edition, its St John's B.6 source statement and ISBN, while documenting controlled rather than open access.

evidence.citation: Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (Brill, 2011).
evidence.locator: Edwards printed pp. 21-30 = PDF pp. 44-53; Liszka printed pp. 31-50 = PDF pp. 54-73; Kline printed pp. 137-157 = PDF pp. 160-180
evidence.summary: Provides modern Laud codicological and temporale context, the pre-1290 terminus for MP, and Pickering's argument that MP was probably a sequel to The Nativity of Mary and Christ and an early temporale Passion narrative. The 365-page provider PDF extracts completely; qpdf's recoverable linearization hint warnings are documented in lineage.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of St John's College, Cambridge (1913), 37-38.
evidence.locator: PDF pp. 61-62; printed pp. 37-38; item 28, B.6
evidence.summary: Supplies B.6's physical description, queried late-fourteenth-century date, complete temporale context, and ff. 25-69v Life of the Virgin and Christ sequence.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, R.3.25.
evidence.locator: contents items 115-117 around f. 270; canvas 571 = f. 270r; canvas 572 = f. 270v
evidence.summary: Confirms the fifteenth-century South English Legendary manuscript and catalogs the Pater Noster as contents item 116 at f. 270 between St Silvester (item 115) and 'Judas was a luþer bryd' (item 117). Direct image inspection shows item 116 beginning around mid-page on f. 270r/canvas 571 after the terminal St Silvester text and continuing only through the upper portion of f. 270v/canvas 572; DIMEV supplies the disputed 52-line and lines-427-472 claims.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00148.
evidence.locator: source entities, primary transmission path, supporting relations, access, rights, editorial practices, and open questions
evidence.summary: Provides the researched separation among the abstract target work, related Magdalene quotation, three target-work manuscript witnesses, Horstmann and Pickering editions, facsimiles, U-M encoding, and repository artifact.

open_questions.question: How do full-work lines 874-2047, the approximately 272-line internal loss, and the reported 901 surviving lines reconcile exactly?
next_steps: Borrow or consult Pickering's 1984 edition, especially pp. 8-10 and the full lineation.
notes: Inclusive arithmetic yields 902, suggesting that a boundary or the rounded loss count is exclusive or approximate.

open_questions.question: Can the Ministry and Passion's place of composition be localized more narrowly than England without transferring a copy's dialect to the abstract work?
next_steps: Consult Pickering's linguistic introduction and later work on South English Legendary temporale dialect and authorship.
notes: West Oxfordshire and Norfolk localizations belong to Laud Hand A and St John's B.6 respectively, not automatically to composition.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV call the Trinity R.3.25 Pater Noster extract 52 lines while mapping it to Ministry and Passion lines 427-472, an inclusive range of 46 positions?
next_steps: Consult Pickering's full 1984 edition and lineation table through controlled access.
Transcribe and align the passage from around mid-page on f. 270r/canvas 571, after the terminal St Silvester text, through the upper portion of f. 270v/canvas 572, stopping before contents item 117.
notes: Canvas 571 is shared with the terminal St Silvester text above item 116; canvas 572 is shared with item 117, so only its upper portion completes the extract before the large red initial opening 'Judas was a luþer bryd.'

open_questions.question: Should St John's B.6 be dated to the queried late fourteenth century or to the early fifteenth century?
next_steps: Consult Pickering's full manuscript description and any newer St John's paleographical cataloging.

notes: The target fragment's intrinsic extent is 901 surviving Laud/CME verbal lines, while the whole encoded-item extent is 959 verbal verse lines after adding the separately modeled 58-line quotation; neither count is the full 3,048-line work or the raw XML L count.
The raw 961 L total consists of 901 target-work lines, two dotted lacuna rows, and one 58-line Life of St Mary Magdalene quotation nested in the footnote to target line 761.
The Laud fragment corresponds to full-work positions 874-2047 but has a substantial internal physical loss after its relative line 90.

The Trinity R.3.25 extract supports the abstract work's transmission without overlapping CME00148's fragment, but its reported 52-line extent conflicts with the inclusive range 427-472 and remains unresolved.
The modern German title, German editorial prose, and brief Latin citations are described at the edition/paratext layer and excluded from work-language discovery facets.
The 58-line Magdalene quotation is linked to DIMEV 4928, Laud ff. 191r-v, Digital Bodleian canvases 397-398, and Horstmann printed pp. 61-62/PDF pp. 73-74.
The closing Latin direction records lost or formerly misbound Passion material and does not establish a second surviving content part.
The Southern Passion is modeled as a later revision of the Ministry and Passion, correcting the older reversed dependency.
No institution-affiliation assumption is made in describing the work or its research value.
